statement: LlamaIndex is a data framework for LLM applications that treats indexing as a first-class citizen: rather than bolting retrieval onto an LLM call, it structures the entire pipeline around ingestion, index construction, and query-time retrieval. This positions it as infrastructure, not a prompt library — the core value proposition is making arbitrary data sources queryable by LLMs with minimal glue code.

LlamaIndex's core object model has three layers: (1) Document — a generic container wrapping any data source (PDF, API response, database row); (2) Node (specifically TextNode, subclassed from BaseNode) — a chunk of a Document that carries text, metadata, and inter-node relationship pointers (prev/next/parent); (3) Index — a structure built over Nodes that enables retrieval (VectorStoreIndex stores each Node plus its embedding, SummaryIndex stores Nodes as a sequential chain, TreeIndex builds a hierarchy, KeywordTableIndex maps keywords to Nodes). NodeParsers bridge Documents to Nodes by chunking and inheriting metadata downward.

LlamaIndex exposes three distinct querying interfaces with different scopes: Retriever fetches the most relevant Nodes from an Index given a query — it is the foundational building block with no synthesis; QueryEngine wraps a Retriever plus a ResponseSynthesizer to answer a single standalone natural-language question in one shot; ChatEngine adds a conversation buffer on top of a QueryEngine, maintaining message history across turns for multi-turn dialogue. Choose Retriever when you own synthesis logic, QueryEngine for single-shot Q&A pipelines, ChatEngine for conversational agents.

VectorStoreIndex, KnowledgeGraphIndex, and PropertyGraphIndex serve fundamentally different retrieval semantics: VectorStoreIndex embeds each Node and retrieves by cosine similarity — best for open-ended semantic search over unstructured text. KnowledgeGraphIndex extracts subject-predicate-object triplets and stores them in a graph — suited for structured entity-relationship queries but is limited in expressiveness and is now considered legacy. PropertyGraphIndex (introduced after KnowledgeGraphIndex) uses a labeled property graph with schema-guided or free-form LLM extraction, supports multiple kg_extractors per chunk, and enables far richer relationship modeling — preferred when graph structure matters. Mixing index types for the same corpus via RouterQueryEngine is a valid production pattern.

LlamaIndex ships over 300 integration packages on PyPI (llama-index-llms-openai, llama-index-embeddings-huggingface, llama-index-vector-stores-chroma, etc.). pip install llama-index installs llama-index-core plus a minimal default set, but does NOT include most integrations. Developers who assume pip install llama-index gives them a working Chroma or HuggingFace embedding setup hit ImportError at runtime. The correct pattern is: install llama-index-core explicitly, then add each integration package individually. Namespace convention distinguishes them: imports containing .core. come from llama-index-core; imports without .core. come from a separate integration package.

The choice of embedding model silently determines retrieval quality in ways LlamaIndex does not surface at runtime. The default embedding model (text-embedding-ada-002, 1536 dimensions) is set globally via Settings.embed_model; if a user loads a pre-built index using one embedding model but queries it with a different one — or changes models between indexing and retrieval — retrieved nodes will be semantically incoherent without any error. Switching to a local model (e.g., HuggingFace BAAI/bge-small-en) changes output dimensionality, causing vector dimension mismatch errors in existing stores. Additionally, ada-002 has been superseded by text-embedding-3-small/large with better retrieval benchmarks at lower cost, but LlamaIndex's default has not always tracked this, leaving new users on a suboptimal default.

FAILURE: The v0.10 package rename from llama_index to llama-index-core broke a massive base of tutorials, blog posts, and Stack Overflow answers. Pre-v0.10 code uses `from llama_index import ...` or `import llama_index`; post-v0.10 the canonical import is `from llama_index.core import ...`. The legacy package llama-index-legacy (pinned to 0.9.48) still exists on PyPI, so pip install llama-index-legacy succeeds silently — meaning a project can have both llama_index (legacy) and llama_index.core (current) installed simultaneously, causing subtle version conflicts. ServiceContext (the pre-v0.10 configuration object) was fully deprecated; its replacement is the module-level Settings singleton. The migration CLI tool (`llamaindex-cli upgrade-file`) exists but is not widely known, leaving most users doing manual find-replace.

FAILURE: LlamaIndex's default chunk_size of 1024 tokens and chunk_overlap of 20 tokens are rarely optimal and produce poor retrieval quality on most real-world corpora without explicit tuning. The 20-token overlap is too small to preserve semantic continuity at chunk boundaries, causing split reasoning chains to be retrieved as incoherent fragments. For dense technical documents, 1024 tokens often exceeds the semantic unit size, embedding noise into vectors and lowering similarity scores for correct matches. The fix is domain-specific: start at 512 tokens with ~50 overlap, then evaluate using a retrieval benchmark. Critically, changing chunk_size on an existing VectorStoreIndex requires full re-indexing — the index does not warn users that stale embeddings from a prior chunk_size are still in use, making silent quality regression a common production failure.

LlamaIndex's IngestionPipeline is the production-grade document processing abstraction that adds deduplication, caching, and transformation chaining on top of raw NodeParsers. It caches transformed nodes by document hash so that re-running an ingestion over unchanged files is a no-op. The UNSAID production pattern: pair IngestionPipeline with a persistent DocStore (SimpleDocumentStore backed to disk, or a database store) and a persistent VectorStore — this enables incremental indexing where only modified documents trigger re-embedding. Without a DocStore, every IngestionPipeline.run() call re-processes all documents regardless of cache, causing redundant embedding API calls and silent cost accumulation on large corpora.

Two-stage retrieval (vector similarity → reranker) dramatically outperforms single-stage vector retrieval for most RAG applications, and LlamaIndex makes this composable via postprocessors. The pattern: retrieve top-K=20-50 candidates from the VectorStoreIndex using fast approximate nearest neighbor search, then rerank with a cross-encoder (CohereRerank, SentenceTransformerRerank, or LLMRerank) to select the final top-k=3-5. Cross-encoders evaluate query-document pairs jointly (vs bi-encoders that embed independently), producing much higher precision at the cost of O(K) inference calls. The UNSAID constraint: LLMRerank (using an LLM to score relevance) is the highest quality but most expensive option; for production cost sensitivity, use a dedicated reranker model (Cohere rerank-english-v3.0, or a local cross-encoder) instead of consuming LLM tokens for reranking.

LlamaIndex's metadata filtering API (MetadataFilter, MetadataFilters) is a portability abstraction over per-vector-store filter syntax, but implementation completeness varies significantly. Pinecone supports all filter operators; Chroma supports a subset; FAISS's in-memory store has no metadata filtering support at all (filters are ignored silently). The failure pattern: a developer builds and tests filtering with Chroma, deploys to FAISS for cost reasons, and discovers that document-type or date-range filters produce no narrowing — all nodes are returned regardless of filter. Always verify metadata filter support for your specific vector store in the LlamaIndex integration docs before building filter-dependent retrieval logic. Use explicit pre-filtering via a retriever wrapper when the vector store does not support native filters.

LlamaIndex QueryEngine uses response synthesis modes that trade quality for cost and latency. `refine` mode sends each retrieved chunk to the LLM sequentially, asking it to refine the answer — highest quality but O(k) LLM calls. `compact` mode consolidates chunks into fewer prompts first, reducing calls by ~50%. `tree_summarize` builds a summary tree and is best for long-document summarization. `simple_summarize` truncates to the context window — lowest cost but silently drops context. `no_text` returns only the source nodes without synthesis — useful when you need raw retrieval output for custom processing. The UNSAID default: QueryEngine defaults to `compact` mode, which is a reasonable production balance. Teams that switch to `refine` for quality without awareness of the O(k) cost multiplication often see 5-10x higher API costs on queries that retrieve many chunks.

LlamaIndex Workflows (introduced in v0.10.x) are an event-driven orchestration primitive that supersedes AgentRunner and custom orchestration logic for multi-step pipelines. Unlike QueryEngine (single-shot Q&A) or AgentRunner (ReAct loop), Workflows define explicit step functions connected by event types, enabling branching, parallelism, and human-in-the-loop interrupts. The WHY this exists: complex RAG pipelines (query decomposition → parallel sub-queries → result fusion → citation extraction) cannot be expressed cleanly as a linear QueryEngine chain. Workflows also integrate with LlamaIndex's instrumentation layer for step-level observability. The migration signal: if your QueryEngine subclass overrides more than one method, rewrite it as a Workflow.

LlamaCloud is a managed platform built on LlamaIndex that handles document parsing, indexing, and retrieval as a service. The critical distinction for self-hosted deployments: some LlamaIndex documentation examples reference `llama_cloud` imports and LlamaCloud-specific readers that require a LlamaCloud API key and send your documents to LlamaAI's servers. This is a data privacy concern for regulated industries. The open-source alternative paths are: use LlamaParse (document parsing) which has a free tier but also sends data to LlamaAI; use completely local parsers (pypdf, docling, unstructured) with the open-source LlamaIndex pipeline. Teams building air-gapped or HIPAA-compliant systems must audit all import paths for `llama_cloud` and `llama_parse` references before deployment.

LlamaIndex query engines and retrievers expose both sync and async APIs (query() vs aquery(), retrieve() vs aretrieve()), but many built-in retrievers and response synthesizers only implement the sync path — the async methods fall back to running sync code in a thread executor. This means calling aquery() in a FastAPI endpoint does not actually free the event loop during LLM I/O; it occupies a thread. To verify whether a component is truly async, check whether it overrides `_aretrieve` or `_aget_response`. True async performance requires using providers and retrievers that implement native async (OpenAI async client, Pinecone async client). Mixing sync-behind-async components in a high-concurrency FastAPI service causes thread pool exhaustion under load.

LlamaIndex Nodes carry relationship pointers (PREVIOUS, NEXT, PARENT, CHILD, SOURCE) that enable context-aware retrieval patterns. The most impactful production technique is 'sentence window retrieval': parse documents into small sentence-level nodes (high retrieval precision), but when a node is retrieved, expand the context window by fetching its PREVIOUS/NEXT neighbors before synthesis. This is implemented via MetadataReplacementPostProcessor which swaps the retrieved node's text with its surrounding window. The UNSAID requirement: window metadata must be embedded into nodes at index time using the SentenceWindowNodeParser — retro-fitting window context onto a pre-built index is not possible without re-indexing. This technique consistently outperforms fixed chunk-size retrieval on benchmarks because it decouples retrieval granularity from synthesis context length.

RAG evaluation requires measuring three independent dimensions: retrieval quality (are the right nodes retrieved?), faithfulness (does the answer stay grounded in the retrieved context?), and answer relevance (does the answer address the question?). LlamaIndex integrates with RAGAS and its own `llama_index.evaluation` module which provides `FaithfulnessEvaluator`, `RelevancyEvaluator`, and `RetrieverEvaluator` using an LLM-as-judge approach. The UNSAID production constraint: LLM-as-judge evaluation costs money and is itself subject to judge model bias — GPT-4o judge scores for GPT-4o-generated answers show systematic favoritism (same-model bias). The correct evaluation baseline is a labeled golden dataset: for each test question, store the ground-truth answer and the expected source node IDs, then measure context recall (fraction of expected nodes retrieved) and exact-match or BERTScore on the answer. Without a golden dataset, you are measuring relative quality between configuration variants, not absolute quality.

ChatEngine has multiple modes that differ fundamentally in how history affects retrieval. `best` mode (CondensePlusContextChatEngine) rewrites the user's current query using conversation history into a standalone question before retrieving — this is the production-recommended mode because retrieval is done on a disambiguated query rather than a bare follow-up like 'tell me more'. `context` mode simply prepends chat history to the retrieved context without query rewriting, causing retrieval to miss relevant documents when the question depends on prior turns. `simple` mode ignores history entirely for retrieval. The UNSAID failure: teams using `context` mode observe that follow-up questions about previously mentioned topics return irrelevant nodes because the bare follow-up question has no semantic overlap with the relevant documents. Switching to `best` mode eliminates this by making one extra LLM call per turn for query condensation — a worthwhile trade-off for conversational applications.

Custom retrievers in LlamaIndex are implemented by subclassing `BaseRetriever` and overriding `_retrieve(query_bundle: QueryBundle) -> list[NodeWithScore]`. QueryBundle contains both the query string and a pre-embedded query vector, enabling the custom retriever to use either for lookup. The UNSAID pattern for combining multiple retrieval strategies: implement a fusion retriever that calls multiple sub-retrievers, deduplicates results by node_id, and applies Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) scoring to combine ranked lists. LlamaIndex ships `QueryFusionRetriever` for this exact case, which also supports query rewriting (generating N paraphrased queries, retrieving for each, then fusing). The critical implementation trap for custom retrievers: `_retrieve` must be synchronous; `_aretrieve` is the async override. Many developers only implement `_retrieve` and assume `aretrieve()` works — it does via thread executor fallback, but this blocks the event loop in async contexts and must be explicitly overridden for truly non-blocking async operation.

Metadata filtering in LlamaIndex VectorStoreIndex improves retrieval precision by pre-filtering the candidate pool before cosine similarity ranking. The correct ingestion pattern is to embed structured metadata at indexing time as Node.metadata dict fields and use `MetadataFilters(filters=[MetadataFilter(key='doc_type', value='contract', operator=FilterOperator.EQ)])` at query time. The UNSAID performance implication: metadata filtering happens at the vector store level (before similarity computation) in databases that support it (Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant) — reducing the search space from millions to thousands of documents before ANN search. For vector stores without native metadata filtering (FAISS, simple in-memory), LlamaIndex applies filtering post-retrieval, which means the full ANN search still runs and then results are dropped — negating the performance benefit. Additionally, injecting metadata into the embedding text using `MetadataMode.EMBED` (which prepends key-value pairs to the text before embedding) can improve recall by making metadata part of the semantic space, useful for filtering on fields the user may express in natural language (date, author, category).

Hybrid search combines dense vector retrieval (semantic similarity) with sparse BM25 retrieval (keyword matching) to cover both exact-match and semantic-match queries. LlamaIndex provides `BM25Retriever` for sparse retrieval over in-memory node sets and `QueryFusionRetriever` with `mode=RECIPROCAL_RERANK` to fuse results. The WHY hybrid outperforms pure vector: vector retrieval fails for queries containing rare proper nouns, product codes, or technical identifiers (e.g., 'CVE-2024-3094') because their embeddings are trained on distribution statistics and rare terms are underrepresented. BM25 retrieves documents by exact term frequency regardless of semantic similarity. The production deployment constraint: LlamaIndex's BM25Retriever builds the BM25 index in-memory from the node corpus at instantiation time — it is not persisted. For large corpora, rebuilding the BM25 index on every service restart is expensive. The solution is to use a vector store that natively supports hybrid search (Weaviate, Qdrant, Elasticsearch) so both retrieval modes share a single persistent index rather than requiring two separate indexes.

LlamaIndex's IngestionPipeline accepts a list of transformations that are applied sequentially: typically [NodeParser, MetadataExtractor(s), EmbeddingModel]. The UNSAID production insight is that transformation order matters: metadata extractors (TitleExtractor, QuestionsAnsweredExtractor, SummaryExtractor) make LLM calls per node and should be placed before the embedding step so that extracted metadata is embedded into the text representation. QuestionsAnsweredExtractor generates hypothetical questions a node answers and appends them to the node's metadata — this improves retrieval by expanding the semantic coverage of each node (a technique called HyDE-adjacent). The cost trap: running QuestionsAnsweredExtractor on 10,000 nodes with GPT-4o at 5 questions/node costs ~$15-50 in API calls for a one-time indexing operation. Use cheaper models (gpt-4o-mini or a local LLM) for metadata extraction, reserving expensive models for answer synthesis at query time.

LlamaIndex's ResponseSynthesizer uses prompt templates for each synthesis step that can be customized via `get_response_synthesizer(text_qa_template=..., refine_template=...)`. The default QA prompt asks the LLM to 'answer the question given the context' without citation or confidence instructions — for production RAG, always override the text_qa_template to (1) require inline citations using node metadata (e.g., 'cite the source document name for each claim'), (2) instruct the model to respond with 'I don't know' when context is insufficient rather than hallucinating, and (3) specify the output format (bullet points, numbered list, prose). The UNSAID failure mode of default prompts: the default refine prompt says 'refine the original answer using the new context' without instructing the model to preserve prior citations, causing the refined answer to drop citations from earlier context windows. Custom refine prompts must explicitly carry citation instructions through every refinement step.

LlamaIndex v0.10 introduced a monorepo split where the core library (llama-index-core) is separate from all integrations (llama-index-llms-openai, llama-index-embeddings-huggingface, etc.). This design enables installing only the dependencies needed for a specific LLM backend, reducing dependency footprint from 50+ packages to under 10. The UNSAID migration trap is that import paths changed entirely: old llama_index.llms.openai becomes llama_index.llms.openai.OpenAI from llama_index.llms.openai, and Settings replaced ServiceContext as the global configuration object. Code written against v0.9 requires systematic import and configuration pattern updates, and the two APIs coexist in pip (both installable) causing version conflict bugs when projects mix old llama-index with new llama-index-core.

LlamaIndex's default chunk_size for SentenceSplitter is 1024 tokens, but the default chunk_overlap is 200 tokens, meaning consecutive chunks share 200 tokens of context. For OpenAI embedding models with a 8192-token limit, this overlap is negligible. However, for smaller models (ada-002 with 8192-token limit, or local models with 512-token limits), the overlap can cause chunks to exceed the embedding model's context window when the base chunk is already near the limit. The correct configuration is to set chunk_size=512 and chunk_overlap=50 for 512-token embedding models, and verify with assert parser.chunk_size + parser.chunk_overlap <= embedding_model.max_tokens. Chunks that exceed the embedding model limit are silently truncated during embedding, causing the end of each chunk (where cross-chunk context is encoded) to be excluded from the embedding representation.

The standard LlamaIndex production ingestion pipeline uses IngestionPipeline with a document store for deduplication and a vector store for persistence. The assembly is: IngestionPipeline(transformations=[SentenceSplitter(chunk_size=512), OpenAIEmbedding()], docstore=SimpleDocumentStore(), vector_store=ChromaVectorStore(chroma_collection)). Setting docstore_strategy=DocstoreStrategy.UPSERTS_AND_DELETE ensures re-ingested documents update existing embeddings rather than duplicating them. Without this strategy, re-ingesting the same document 5 times produces 5 copies of all its chunks in the vector store, degrading retrieval quality by overwhelming results with duplicate content. The pipeline.run(documents=...) call is idempotent when docstore is configured: documents are fingerprinted by their doc_id, and only changed documents trigger re-embedding.

LlamaIndex's VectorStoreIndex.from_documents() builds the index in-memory by default, meaning all document embeddings are stored in RAM as a numpy array. For large document corpora (>100k documents), this causes OOM because numpy arrays for 100k 1536-dimension OpenAI embeddings require 100k * 1536 * 4 bytes = 614MB of RAM minimum, plus Python object overhead. The correct production approach is to pass a vector_store parameter pointing to a persistent vector database (Chroma, Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant): VectorStoreIndex.from_documents(docs, vector_store=chroma_store). With a persistent vector store, from_documents() inserts embeddings directly to the store without holding them all in RAM simultaneously. The in-memory default is appropriate for prototyping with <10k documents but silently fails at scale due to memory exhaustion that manifests as slow performance (swap) before OOM crash.

LlamaIndex's RetrieverQueryEngine uses similarity_top_k=2 by default, returning only 2 chunks for any query regardless of document count or query complexity. For knowledge bases with many relevant documents, this severely limits recall: the correct answer may require synthesizing information from 5-10 chunks, but only 2 are retrieved and passed to the LLM. Teams often discover this when their RAG system gives incomplete answers on questions that require cross-document reasoning. The correct production configuration depends on LLM context window size: for GPT-4-turbo with 128k context, similarity_top_k=20 is feasible (retrieves more context); for GPT-3.5-turbo with 16k context, similarity_top_k=5-8 with reranking is appropriate. Use the Cohere reranker (CohereRerank) or cross-encoder (sentence-transformers cross-encoder) as a second-stage to select the most relevant k' chunks from the top-k retrieved, maintaining quality while managing context window cost.

LlamaIndex supports hybrid retrieval combining dense (vector) and sparse (BM25) search via QueryFusionRetriever, which runs both retrieval methods and merges results using Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF). The assembly is: bm25_retriever = BM25Retriever.from_defaults(index, similarity_top_k=10); vector_retriever = index.as_retriever(similarity_top_k=10); hybrid = QueryFusionRetriever([bm25_retriever, vector_retriever], similarity_top_k=5, mode='reciprocal_rerank'). Hybrid retrieval consistently outperforms pure vector retrieval by 5-15% on factual Q&A benchmarks because BM25 handles exact keyword matching (names, codes, IDs) where semantic search is weak, while vector search handles paraphrase and conceptual similarity. The BM25Retriever requires the llama-index-retrievers-bm25 package and builds the BM25 index in-memory from the document text, so it does not require a separate search backend.

LlamaIndex's Settings object (introduced in v0.10) is a global singleton that configures the default LLM, embedding model, chunk size, and callback manager for all LlamaIndex operations in the current process. This replaces ServiceContext which was passed explicitly to every index and query engine constructor. The UNSAID consequence is that Settings is not thread-safe for concurrent requests with different LLM configurations: changing Settings.llm in one request handler affects all concurrent requests. For multi-tenant applications where different users have different LLM configurations (different API keys, different models), the correct approach is to use explicit constructors (index.as_query_engine(llm=per_user_llm)) rather than relying on Settings, because Settings is a shared global that leaks configuration across requests.

LlamaIndex's SubQuestionQueryEngine decomposes a complex question into sub-questions, queries each sub-document separately, and synthesizes a final answer. The failure mode is that sub-question generation relies on the LLM choosing the right data sources for each sub-question, and for queries that span multiple document types, the LLM often generates sub-questions that query the wrong document collection. For example, asking 'compare the pricing of product A and product B' may generate sub-questions that both query the same product's documentation. The diagnostic is to enable query logging with CallbackManager and inspect the generated sub-questions. The fix is to provide detailed tool descriptions in QueryEngineTool that clearly differentiate what each data source contains, using specific metadata (product names, date ranges, document types) rather than generic descriptions like 'contains company information'.

The standard LlamaIndex pattern for multi-document question answering over a large corpus is RouterQueryEngine with per-document summary index: (1) create a SummaryIndex per document with a one-sentence summary stored as the tool description; (2) wrap each in a QueryEngineTool with the summary as the tool description; (3) RouterQueryEngine selects the most relevant tools for each query using the LLM to match query against tool descriptions. This pattern scales to hundreds of documents because the router only calls each document's query engine when its summary is relevant, avoiding exhaustive search across all documents. The critical implementation detail is that tool descriptions must be stored persistently (in a JSON file or database) alongside the index; re-generating summaries on every startup via SummaryIndex.summary is an LLM call that costs money and time proportional to corpus size.

LlamaIndex's PropertyGraphIndex (introduced in v0.10.30) enables graph-based retrieval by extracting entity-relation triplets from documents and storing them in a property graph. The critical constraint is that triplet extraction uses the LLM to parse (subject, predicate, object) from each chunk, making ingestion 5-10x more expensive than vector-only indexing. For a 1000-page corpus, vector-only indexing costs ~$2 in OpenAI API calls; graph extraction costs $10-20 depending on chunk size. The graph extraction quality also depends heavily on chunk size: chunks that are too small (< 200 tokens) lack enough context for meaningful relationship extraction, while chunks that are too large (> 1000 tokens) cause the LLM to miss relationships that span sentence boundaries. The optimal chunk size for graph extraction is 400-600 tokens with 50-token overlap.

LlamaIndex's default node relationship tracking (prev_node, next_node, parent_node) enables context enrichment at query time: when a relevant node is retrieved, its neighboring nodes can be fetched for additional context via NodeWithScore's node.relationships dict. However, this relationship tracking is only preserved when the document is indexed using SentenceSplitter or HierarchicalNodeParser; using a custom chunking function that creates nodes without setting the relationships attribute produces nodes with empty relationship dicts. Downstream code that relies on node.prev_node to fetch surrounding context will silently receive None and produce answer quality degradation without raising exceptions. Teams that implement custom chunkers must explicitly set node.relationships[NodeRelationship.PREVIOUS] = RelatedNodeInfo(node_id=prev_node.node_id) to maintain the relationship graph expected by context-aware retrievers.

LlamaIndex Workflows (introduced in v0.10.38) provide a declarative event-driven pipeline system for complex multi-step AI workflows with built-in retry logic, parallel execution, and streaming support. A Workflow is defined by decorator-annotated steps: @step(input=StartEvent) async def parse_docs(ev: StartEvent): ...; each step emits typed Events to trigger downstream steps. Unlike simple pipelines, Workflows support branching (one step can emit different event types to trigger different downstream paths), parallel fan-out (multiple downstream steps triggered by one event), and fan-in (a step waits for multiple upstream events before executing). The key production advantage over raw async code is that Workflow execution state is serializable: a running workflow can be paused, serialized to JSON, stored in Redis, and resumed in a different process, enabling long-running workflows that span multiple API request/response cycles.

The recommended LlamaIndex pattern for production RAG evaluation is to use Ragas or TruLens for automated quality measurement across three dimensions: faithfulness (does the answer stay grounded in retrieved context?), answer relevancy (does the answer address the question?), and context precision (are retrieved chunks actually relevant?). The integration is: wrap the query engine with TruLlama(query_engine, app_id='prod-v1', feedbacks=[Groundedness(), AnswerRelevance(), ContextRelevance()]); every query and response is logged to a SQLite database with automated metric computation. The TruLens dashboard shows drift in quality metrics across deployments, enabling immediate rollback detection when a new model or retrieval configuration degrades RAG quality. Without automated quality measurement, RAG systems degrade silently as document corpus changes or LLM API updates alter response behavior.

LlamaIndex's SummaryIndex (formerly ListIndex) synthesizes responses by iterating over ALL nodes in the index and asking the LLM to refine its answer incrementally, which makes it O(n) in LLM calls where n is the number of nodes. For a document with 50 chunks, SummaryIndex makes 50+ LLM calls per query (one per chunk in the refine chain), costing 50x more than a VectorStoreIndex query. Teams that use SummaryIndex for large documents expecting it to be fast like VectorStoreIndex are surprised by 30-60 second query times and high API costs. SummaryIndex is appropriate only for small documents (< 10 chunks) where comprehensive synthesis across the entire document is required. For larger documents, use VectorStoreIndex with similarity_top_k=5 for selective retrieval, or TreeIndex which builds a hierarchical summary tree that reduces synthesis to O(log n) LLM calls.

The standard LlamaIndex pattern for multi-modal RAG (combining text and image retrieval) is MultiModalVectorStoreIndex which stores both text embeddings (via OpenAIEmbedding) and image embeddings (via OpenAICLIPEmbedding or LlavaImageEmbedding) in separate vector stores with unified retrieval. The assembly: mm_index = MultiModalVectorStoreIndex.from_documents(docs, image_documents=image_docs); mm_retriever = mm_index.as_retriever(similarity_top_k=3, image_similarity_top_k=3). The retrieved results contain both text NodeWithScore and ImageNode objects, which are passed to a vision LLM (GPT-4V or LLaVA) for synthesis. The critical assembly constraint is that image documents must have their file_path metadata set to the actual image file path (not a URL), because the synthesis step reads the image bytes from disk rather than downloading from the web. URL-based images require downloading to a temporary directory before indexing.

LlamaIndex's CallbackManager and event tracing system (LlamaDebugHandler) is the primary tool for debugging pipeline internals, but it has a memory leak in long-running applications: the LlamaDebugHandler stores ALL events in memory indefinitely by default, with no eviction policy. In a production service handling thousands of queries per day, this causes gradual memory growth that leads to OOM after days or weeks of operation. The correct production setup is either: (1) disable the debug handler and use only WandbCallbackHandler or ArizeCallbackHandler which ship events to external services rather than storing in-memory; or (2) periodically call debug_handler.flush() to clear the event buffer. The event buffer can grow to hundreds of MB for high-traffic applications because each query stores full node content and LLM prompts/responses in memory.

LlamaIndex's document metadata filtering (MetadataFilters) allows vector store queries to filter by metadata fields before similarity search, but the metadata field names and types must exactly match what was stored during ingestion. A common failure is storing metadata as {'date': datetime.date(2024, 1, 15)} during ingestion (Python date object) and then filtering with MetadataFilter(key='date', value='2024-01-15') (string) during query time. Most vector stores serialize metadata values differently for different Python types, so the stored representation of a date object may be a timestamp integer, not a string. The filter comparison then fails silently: zero results are returned instead of a type conversion error. The correct pattern is to standardize all metadata values to JSON-serializable primitives (strings, numbers, booleans) during document creation via Document(metadata={'date': '2024-01-15', 'year': 2024}) and use consistent types between ingestion and filter time.

LlamaIndex's HierarchicalNodeParser creates a three-tier node hierarchy: chunk_sizes=[2048, 512, 128] produces parent nodes (2048 tokens), child nodes (512 tokens), and leaf nodes (128 tokens). At query time, AutoMergingRetriever retrieves leaf nodes by similarity, then promotes retrieved leaf nodes to their parent level if a majority of siblings are also retrieved (indicating the broader context is relevant). This design solves the precision-recall tradeoff in RAG: small chunks improve retrieval precision (less noise in retrieved context), while the merge-up-to-parent mechanism provides comprehensive context when the topic is consistently relevant across multiple small chunks. The key tuning parameter is the merge threshold (leaf_nodes_retrieved >= total_leaf_nodes * threshold, default 0.5): lower threshold promotes parents more aggressively (more context, more cost); higher threshold keeps granular leaf nodes.

LlamaIndex's persist() and load_index_from_storage() pattern saves index metadata (node IDs, chunk metadata) to JSON files, but does NOT persist the vector store embeddings when using the default SimpleVectorStore. The embeddings are saved to default_vector_store.json, which is only written when storage_context.persist(persist_dir) is called on the StorageContext, not on the index directly. Teams that call index.storage_context.persist() but then load with StorageContext.from_defaults(persist_dir=persist_dir) often discover their embeddings are missing if the vector_store.json file was not included in the persist_dir or was corrupted. The complete persistence check is: verify persist_dir contains docstore.json, index_store.json, AND default_vector_store.json before considering the persist successful. Missing any one of these three files causes incomplete index reconstruction that raises AttributeError or returns zero retrieval results.

The recommended LlamaIndex pattern for real-time document updates (when the source corpus changes frequently) is a differential ingestion pipeline: track document modification timestamps, re-ingest only modified documents, and use docstore deduplication to update existing embeddings in place. The implementation uses RefDocInfo to track which nodes belong to each source document: index.ref_doc_info returns a dict mapping doc_id to RefDocInfo(node_ids=[...], metadata={...}). When a document is updated, delete old nodes via index.delete_ref_doc(doc_id, delete_from_docstore=True) before inserting new nodes. This delete-insert pattern is safer than upsert for vector stores that do not support true upsert semantics (many vector stores create duplicate vectors on update rather than replacing). Schedule the differential ingestion check hourly or on file system change events using inotify or fsevents.

LlamaIndex's SQLJoinQueryEngine combines structured SQL query results with unstructured vector retrieval via a two-phase approach: first, the LLM generates SQL to retrieve structured data from the relational database; second, the SQL results are used as context to query the vector index for supplementary unstructured information. The assembly requires NLSQLTableQueryEngine (for the structured component) and VectorStoreIndex.as_query_engine() (for the unstructured component), wrapped by SQLJoinQueryEngine. The practical use case is hybrid analytics: 'Show me the top 5 customers by revenue (SQL) and summarize their recent support tickets (vector search)'. The capability constraint is that the LLM must generate syntactically correct SQL for the target database schema, which requires accurate table and column descriptions in the SQL index setup. Using SQLDatabase.from_uri() with include_tables parameter limits LLM visibility to only relevant tables, reducing SQL generation errors.

LlamaIndex's async query execution (await query_engine.aquery()) does not provide true parallelism for LLM API calls unless the underlying LLM client uses async HTTP. The OpenAI async client (AsyncOpenAI) is used by default when await is called, providing true async I/O for OpenAI API calls. However, if a custom LLM or embedding model is used that wraps a synchronous HTTP client (e.g., Anthropic SDK sync client), calling aquery() runs the LLM calls in an executor thread pool rather than truly async, providing concurrency but not the zero-overhead async I/O benefit. The practical constraint for LlamaIndex + FastAPI integration: ensure all LLM and embedding components use async clients to avoid blocking the asyncio event loop during LLM calls. Wrap sync LLM clients with await asyncio.get_event_loop().run_in_executor(None, sync_llm_call) rather than calling them directly in async handlers.

LlamaIndex's citation query engine (CitationQueryEngine) adds source attribution to responses by inserting node metadata (source document, page number) into the synthesized response. However, the citation format relies on the LLM correctly inserting citation markers like [1], [2] in the response text that correspond to the source list. For LLMs with weak instruction following, the citations are omitted or misplaced: the LLM generates a response without citation markers, or inserts markers that do not correspond to the actual source nodes. The symptom is responses with no citations or with citations pointing to the wrong nodes. The fix is to use a more explicit system prompt that requires citations via a structured response format, or to switch to a post-hoc citation approach that uses NER to detect claims and then matches each claim to its source node via similarity search rather than relying on the LLM to self-cite.

The standard LlamaIndex pattern for handling tabular data (CSV, Excel) in RAG is PandasQueryEngine for small tables (<10k rows) or a combination of PandasQueryEngine + VectorStoreIndex for large tables: use PandasQueryEngine for data queries (aggregations, filters, statistics) and VectorStoreIndex on row text representations for semantic search. PandasQueryEngine generates Python/pandas code from natural language queries and executes it against the DataFrame, enabling queries like 'average revenue by region' without SQL. The security critical constraint is that PandasQueryEngine executes generated Python code via exec() — this is a code execution vulnerability if user queries can contain adversarial content. In production, always sanitize the generated code via a code review step or use PandasQueryEngine in a sandboxed environment (Docker with no network access, restricted filesystem permissions) to prevent prompt injection leading to data exfiltration.

LlamaIndex separates the concepts of Retriever (fetches relevant Nodes) and ResponseSynthesizer (generates the final answer from retrieved Nodes + query) to enable independent optimization of each component. Response synthesizers implement different strategies: Refine (iteratively refines a single answer across all retrieved nodes, O(n) LLM calls), CompactAndRefine (packs nodes into fewer prompts before refining, reduces LLM calls by 2-3x vs Refine), TreeSummarize (builds a summary tree bottom-up, O(log n) LLM calls, best for summarization), and Simple (single LLM call with all nodes concatenated, fastest but hits context limit quickly). The UNSAID design implication is that synthesis mode should be chosen based on: Refine for high accuracy with few nodes (<5); CompactAndRefine for standard production use; TreeSummarize for long document summarization; Simple only when nodes fit in context and minimal latency is required.

LlamaIndex's sentence window retrieval (SentenceWindowNodeParser + MetadataReplacementPostProcessor) stores each sentence as a separate node with its surrounding window (default 3 sentences on each side) in the node's metadata, then replaces the retrieved sentence with its full window context before synthesis. The failure mode is when the window sentences contain metadata from different sections of a document (headers, footers, page numbers) that were chunked alongside content sentences. When these non-content sentences are retrieved and their windows expanded, the window context contains irrelevant header/footer text that confuses the LLM synthesis step. The fix is to preprocess documents to remove headers, footers, and page numbers before indexing, or to use a custom metadata replacer that filters out non-content sentences from windows via a simple heuristic (sentences < 5 words or consisting only of numbers/special chars are likely non-content).

The recommended LlamaIndex pattern for multi-hop reasoning (questions requiring chaining 2+ retrieval steps) is agent-based query decomposition using ReActAgent or FunctionCallingAgent with multiple QueryEngineTools. Each tool represents a different knowledge domain or query type. The agent's LLM decides which tools to call in sequence, passing intermediate results as context for subsequent tool calls. The key implementation constraint is that each tool's description must specify its INPUT format (what kind of questions it can answer), OUTPUT format (what kind of information it returns), and LIMITATIONS (what it cannot answer), enabling the agent to chain tools correctly. For example: 'Use this tool with a company name to get their financial data as a JSON dict with keys: revenue, employees, founded_year'. Without explicit output format descriptions, the agent cannot effectively chain tools because it does not know how to interpret intermediate results to form the next query.

LlamaIndex's Pinecone vector store integration (PineconeVectorStore) requires that the Pinecone index be created with the exact embedding dimension before use: creating the Pinecone index with dimension=1536 for OpenAI ada-002 embeddings and then switching to OpenAI text-embedding-3-large (3072 dimensions) causes pinecone.exceptions.PineconeException: Vector dimension 3072 does not match the dimension of the index 1536. This is not a LlamaIndex error but a Pinecone constraint: the vector dimension is set at index creation time and cannot be changed. The correct production migration path when switching embedding models is to create a new Pinecone index with the correct dimension, re-ingest all documents with the new embedding model, and update the application to use the new index. There is no in-place migration path. Teams must plan for this migration cost (re-embedding entire corpus) when evaluating embedding model upgrades.

LlamaIndex's QueryEngineTool wraps a query engine with a name and description for use by agents. A critical failure mode is that the tool description is used verbatim as the tool spec in the LLM's tool selection prompt, and descriptions longer than ~200 words cause token budget issues in the agent's planning prompt when many tools are registered simultaneously. With 20 tools each having 300-word descriptions, the agent's context window is consumed by tool specs (6000 tokens) before any conversation history or query context is included. The LLM then generates suboptimal tool selections because it lacks context about the current query. The fix is to use concise 1-3 sentence tool descriptions (max 50 words) focused on what the tool does and what unique information it accesses, rather than comprehensive API documentation. The detailed documentation should be in the system prompt that the agent uses for final synthesis, not in the tool spec.

The standard LlamaIndex assembly pattern for conversational RAG with memory is: (1) CondensePlusContextChatEngine which rewrites the user question in context of conversation history before retrieval; (2) ChatMemoryBuffer with token_limit=4096 for conversation history management (automatically truncates oldest messages when limit is reached); (3) VectorStoreIndex.as_retriever with similarity_top_k=5; (4) custom text_qa_template that includes both retrieved context and conversation summary. CondensePlusContextChatEngine addresses the common failure of naive RAG chatbots that retrieve based on the raw current question without considering previous context: 'What was her age again?' retrieves nothing useful without knowing who 'her' refers to from prior conversation. The condensed question rewrite step (e.g., 'What is Marie Curie's age?' from context) enables semantically correct retrieval for conversational follow-up questions.

LlamaIndex chose to implement document loaders as SimpleDirectoryReader (single directory) and specialized readers per file type (PDFReader, DocxReader, etc.) rather than a single unified file loader, because different file types require fundamentally different parsing strategies that cannot be abstracted without quality loss. PDFs can be scanned images (requiring OCR) or native text (requiring layout-aware extraction); DOCX files have styles and track changes; HTML requires link resolution and element filtering. The UNSAID consequence is that OCR-requiring PDFs must use LlamaParse (cloud service) or PyMuPDF with tesseract-ocr installed locally, not the default PDFReader (which only handles native text PDFs). Teams that index scanned PDF collections with the default PDFReader receive documents containing only whitespace or garbled characters, and the index appears complete but retrieves meaningless content for all queries.

LlamaIndex's VectorIndexRetriever does not guarantee that metadata fields excluded from the exclude_llm_metadata_keys list are included in the retrieval result nodes. When metadata_filters are applied, the filter works on the vector store's stored metadata, but the returned NodeWithScore.node.metadata may contain only a subset of the original metadata if the vector store limits the metadata size it stores per vector (Pinecone limits metadata to 40KB per vector, Chroma limits to 1MB). Large metadata values (base64-encoded images stored as metadata, full document text cached in metadata) can be silently truncated, causing downstream code that reads node.metadata['full_text'] to receive an empty string or truncated string. The correct production pattern is to store only indexed/searchable metadata (title, date, author, category) in the vector store metadata, and fetch the full document content from a separate document store (SimpleDocumentStore or database) by node_id when the full content is needed.

The recommended LlamaIndex pattern for question answering over code repositories is: (1) use CodeSplitter (from llama-index-utils-workflow) to split code files by function/class boundaries rather than token count; (2) enrich each node with metadata including file_path, function_name, class_name, and docstring; (3) use VectorStoreIndex with code-specific embeddings (voyage-code-2 or text-embedding-3-large with 1536 dimensions which performs well on code); (4) at query time, filter by file extension metadata to scope retrieval to relevant languages. CodeSplitter uses tree-sitter for language-aware parsing, preserving function and class boundaries that token-count chunkers would split mid-function. The critical advantage is that retrieved chunks correspond to complete logical units (full functions, full classes) rather than arbitrary token windows, dramatically improving LLM synthesis quality for code understanding tasks.

LlamaIndex's FewShotAgent enables in-context learning for query planning: past successful (query, tool_calls, answer) triplets are stored as examples and automatically retrieved from a few-shot store when a new query arrives. The most similar past query examples are injected into the agent's planning prompt, guiding tool selection and sequencing for the new query. This capability is particularly valuable for specialized domains where the LLM lacks domain-specific knowledge about which tools to call in which order. The practical implementation uses a SemanticSimilarityExemplarsStore backed by a VectorStoreIndex: past successful queries are embedded and retrieved by similarity at query time, with the top-k most similar examples injected into the system prompt. The few-shot store must be maintained by humans initially (10-50 hand-curated examples) and can be augmented automatically from production queries rated as high quality by users.

LlamaIndex's Weaviate vector store integration requires the Weaviate class schema to be pre-created with matching properties before inserting nodes. LlamaIndex does NOT auto-create Weaviate schema from node metadata; attempting to insert nodes with metadata fields not defined in the Weaviate schema raises weaviate.exceptions.UnexpectedStatusCodeException: 422 with a non-obvious error message about invalid properties. The correct setup flow is: (1) inspect node metadata keys after parsing; (2) create Weaviate schema with those exact property names and appropriate data types; (3) connect WeaviateVectorStore to the pre-created class; (4) run ingestion. A second constraint is that Weaviate property names must match Weaviate's naming conventions (camelCase, no underscores at the start) while LlamaIndex metadata keys are often snake_case (file_name, page_number). Teams must normalize metadata key names to camelCase before indexing or use a metadata mapper at the WeaviateVectorStore configuration layer.

LlamaIndex's streaming response (query_engine.query() with streaming=True returns a StreamingResponse object) does not expose completion tokens or usage statistics in the stream, because OpenAI's streaming API does not include usage data in intermediate SSE chunks (only in the final chunk for newer API versions). Code that accumulates streamed tokens and counts them to track API costs will undercount actual usage because LlamaIndex's StreamingResponse concatenates chunk texts but does not expose the usage field from the final chunk. The correct cost tracking pattern for streaming queries is to use CallbackManager with a TokenCountingHandler: tokenizer = tiktoken.encoding_for_model('gpt-4'); token_counter = TokenCountingHandler(tokenizer=tokenizer); after query, check token_counter.total_llm_token_count which counts both prompt and completion tokens via the tiktoken tokenizer, providing accurate cost estimates without relying on the incomplete streaming usage field.

The standard LlamaIndex pattern for knowledge base Q&A with access control (per-user document visibility) is per-user metadata filtering combined with per-session index views. Each document is tagged with user_id or role metadata during ingestion. At query time, MetadataFilters(filters=[MetadataFilter(key='allowed_roles', value=user_role, operator=FilterOperator.CONTAINS)]) restricts retrieval to documents accessible to the current user. For fine-grained row-level security, Qdrant's payload indexing or Pinecone's metadata filtering provide efficient server-side filtering before similarity search. The security contract is that metadata filters are applied BEFORE returning results to the application layer, preventing the application from seeing unauthorized content. Teams must verify that the vector store's metadata filtering is enforced server-side (not client-side after retrieval), because client-side filtering receives all documents including unauthorized ones, exposing them to memory inspection.

LlamaIndex's Arize Phoenix integration (Arize AI's open-source observability platform) provides the most comprehensive tracing for production RAG pipelines. Phoenix traces each query through all pipeline stages: embedding lookup, vector retrieval (showing which documents and similarity scores), prompt construction (showing the exact prompt sent to the LLM), and final response. This end-to-end trace visibility enables identifying where in the pipeline quality degrades: poor retrieval (wrong documents retrieved), poor synthesis (correct documents but LLM fails to extract the answer), or poor chunking (answer split across chunk boundaries). The integration is: phoenix.launch_app(); from llama_index.callbacks.arize_phoenix import set_global_handler; set_global_handler('arize_phoenix'). No code changes to the LlamaIndex pipeline are needed; the handler instruments all LLM and embedding calls automatically via the callback system.

LlamaIndex's document metadata is included in the LLM prompt by default when text_qa_template is used, with all metadata key-value pairs prepended to the node text. For documents with many metadata fields (20+ fields including internal tracking IDs, timestamps, processing flags), the metadata section of each retrieved chunk can consume 200-500 tokens of the LLM's context window, reducing the available space for actual document content and the user's question. The fix is to configure exclude_llm_metadata_keys in the node's metadata_template or to use SentenceSplitter with metadata_seperator configuration. A second approach is to use node.excluded_llm_metadata_keys = list of keys to exclude at index time. Teams should measure the token overhead of metadata for their dataset and set a policy: include only metadata that the LLM actually uses for synthesis (source_title, date, author), exclude internal metadata (processing_timestamp, chunk_id, fingerprint).

LlamaIndex's query transformation layer (QueryTransform, HyDEQueryTransform, StepDecomposeQueryTransform) modifies the user's query before retrieval to improve recall. HyDEQueryTransform (Hypothetical Document Embeddings) generates a hypothetical answer to the query using the LLM and uses that hypothetical answer as the retrieval query, exploiting the observation that a hypothetical answer embedding is closer to relevant document embeddings than the original question embedding. The design rationale is that questions and answers exist in different semantic spaces in embedding models: 'What is the capital of France?' is semantically different from 'Paris is the capital of France', but the latter is much closer in embedding space to actual documents about Paris. HyDE improves recall by 10-20% on factual Q&A benchmarks at the cost of one additional LLM call per query. The latency overhead (100-500ms for the HyDE LLM call) must be weighed against the recall improvement for latency-sensitive applications.

The recommended LlamaIndex pattern for building a customer support knowledge base that must be kept current with product documentation updates is event-driven re-indexing: trigger document re-ingestion when source files change via filesystem watchers (watchdog library on Linux/macOS) or object storage event notifications (S3 Event Notifications, GCS Pub/Sub). The pipeline: file change event -> SimpleDirectoryReader.load_data() -> compare document hash against stored hash -> if changed, delete old ref_doc and insert new nodes -> update hash store. Crucially, test the re-indexing latency end-to-end: for large files that split into 500+ chunks, re-ingestion requires 500+ embedding API calls taking 30-60 seconds. If near-real-time freshness (< 30 seconds) is required, pre-chunk documents and cache embeddings at the chunk level, only re-embedding chunks that actually changed rather than all chunks of a modified document.

LlamaIndex's Qdrant vector store integration requires specifying the collection name and vector size at connection time, and the collection must have a matching dense vector field named 'text-sparse' for hybrid search or a single unnamed dense vector for pure vector search depending on the configuration. When using QdrantVectorStore with enable_hybrid=True, Qdrant's sparse vector index must be explicitly created on the collection: client.create_payload_index() call before inserting nodes. Without the sparse index, hybrid=True queries fall back to pure dense search without warning, silently ignoring the BM25 component. Teams enabling hybrid search should verify both dense and sparse components are active by checking that query results include both 'score' (dense) and 'sparse_score' fields in the Qdrant response payload. The combination of Qdrant + BM25 hybrid with LlamaIndex requires qdrant-client >= 1.7 and the fastembed library for sparse vector generation.

LlamaIndex's JSONalyzeQueryEngine and JSONQueryEngine handle structured JSON data by converting JSON to a string representation and querying it with LLM prompts. For nested JSON structures (API responses with 5+ levels of nesting) or large JSON arrays (1000+ items), the string representation exceeds LLM context limits and produces truncated query results. The JSONQueryEngine does NOT automatically paginate through large JSON data; it simply passes the full JSON string to the LLM and receives whatever fits in the context window. For large JSON datasets, the correct approach is to flatten the JSON to tabular format (using pd.json_normalize()) and use PandasQueryEngine instead, which handles tabular data more efficiently through pandas operations rather than string-based LLM reasoning. Alternatively, extract the relevant JSON fields into discrete Document objects with metadata, using standard VectorStoreIndex for semantic search over JSON content.

The standard LlamaIndex pattern for production vector store migration (moving from one vector store backend to another, e.g., Chroma to Pinecone as corpus grows) without downtime is: (1) run new Pinecone ingestion pipeline in parallel with existing Chroma serving; (2) once Pinecone ingestion completes, run shadow-mode validation (compare top-k results from both stores for 100 sample queries); (3) switch traffic to Pinecone atomically via a feature flag; (4) keep Chroma running for 24 hours as rollback target; (5) decommission Chroma after validation period. The critical risk in step 2 is that Pinecone and Chroma may have slightly different result orderings for the same query due to approximate nearest neighbor algorithm differences (HNSW vs. Pinecone's proprietary index), causing apparent quality differences that are actually algorithm-level differences rather than data integrity issues. Set a threshold: if top-1 match agrees in >90% of test queries, the migration is valid.

LlamaIndex's KnowledgeGraphIndex (using Neo4j or TigerGraph backends) stores entity-relationship triplets extracted from documents and enables graph traversal queries in addition to semantic similarity search. The GraphRAG pattern combines vector retrieval with graph traversal: retrieve relevant entities via semantic similarity, then traverse the knowledge graph 1-2 hops from those entities to discover related entities not directly in the retrieved chunks. This is particularly valuable for reasoning about multi-entity relationships: 'What companies compete with Company X in the European market?' can be answered by traversing the CompetesIn and LocatedIn edges from the Company X entity node, finding entities that share those edges. The key capability that pure vector RAG lacks is explicit relationship-based retrieval: vector similarity finds contextually similar text, but graph traversal finds structurally related entities even when their text is not semantically similar to the query.

LlamaIndex's OpenAI-based LLM (OpenAI class from llama-index-llms-openai) applies automatic retry with exponential backoff for rate limit errors (429) by default. However, the default max_retries=10 with exponential backoff can cause a single query to block for up to 512 seconds (2^10 = 1024 seconds cap) before failing, hanging the web server thread for the entire retry window. For user-facing applications, the correct configuration is max_retries=3 with a total timeout of 30 seconds: OpenAI(max_retries=3, timeout=30.0). The underlying httpx client timeout must also be set: httpx_async_client=AsyncClient(timeout=Timeout(30.0)). Without explicit timeout configuration, a single OpenAI API connection that stalls (network issue) can block a server thread for the httpx default 5-minute timeout, exhausting the thread pool for all concurrent requests.

The recommended LlamaIndex pattern for handling multi-language documents in a single index is to store the detected language as node metadata and use language-specific embedding models via a routing embedding wrapper: documents in English use text-embedding-3-large, documents in Chinese use multilingual-e5-large, documents in other languages use multilingual-e5-base as fallback. The routing wrapper: class LanguageRoutingEmbedding(BaseEmbedding): def _get_text_embedding(self, text): lang = detect(text); model = lang_to_model_map.get(lang, default_model); return model.get_text_embedding(text). The critical constraint is that all documents in the same index must use the same embedding dimension (otherwise vector similarity comparison is meaningless). Use a multilingual model with consistent dimensions (1024 for multilingual-e5-large) as the universal model when language routing is too complex, sacrificing per-language quality for index consistency.

LlamaIndex's postprocessors (MetadataReplacementPostProcessor, SentenceTransformerRerank, LLMRerank) execute sequentially in the order they are added to the QueryEngine or Retriever, but the order matters significantly for quality and cost. The correct order is: (1) fast metadata filtering and replacement postprocessors first; (2) cross-encoder reranking second (operates on the filtered set); (3) LLM-based reranking last (most expensive, operates on the pre-reranked top-n). Placing LLMRerank before cross-encoder reranking inverts the intended cost pyramid: the expensive LLM reranker processes the full retrieval set (50+ nodes) instead of the pre-filtered top-10. LLMRerank is designed to choose the final top-3 from a pre-filtered set of 10 candidates, not to filter 50 candidates. The concrete configuration: query_engine = index.as_query_engine(node_postprocessors=[MetadataFilter(...), SentenceTransformerRerank(top_n=10), LLMRerank(top_n=3)]).

LlamaIndex's token budget enforcement via TokenLimitReranker truncates the retrieved node list to fit within a token budget, but it counts tokens using the tokenizer passed at initialization (default: tiktoken cl100k_base for GPT-4/3.5). If the actual LLM being used has a different tokenizer (LLaMA-3's tiktoken llama3 encoding, or a locally served model), the token count estimate is inaccurate. For LLaMA-3, GPT-4's cl100k_base tokenizer counts approximately 10-15% fewer tokens than the actual LLaMA-3 tokenizer for technical text, causing TokenLimitReranker to include more nodes than actually fit in LLaMA-3's context, resulting in context window overflow. The correct production configuration is to initialize TokenLimitReranker with the exact tokenizer of the deployed LLM: for LLaMA-3, use tiktoken.encoding_for_model('gpt-4') only as an approximation, or use the transformers tokenizer for the exact count.

LlamaIndex's distinction between Index (persistent data structure) and QueryEngine (stateless query processor) follows the separation of concerns principle: indexes are expensive to build (requiring embedding API calls) and should be built once and reused; query engines are cheap to instantiate and should be created fresh per request (or per session) to avoid state sharing. The practical consequence is that index.as_query_engine() should be called within the request handler with request-specific configuration (user-specific LLM, user-specific filters) rather than at application startup with global configuration. The index object itself (VectorStoreIndex, SummaryIndex) should be a module-level singleton loaded from persistent storage at startup, while the query engine wrapping it is ephemeral. This pattern correctly separates the expensive IO-bound operation (index loading) from the cheap configuration-binding operation (query engine creation).

LlamaIndex's structured output extraction via LLM.structured_predict(OutputClass, prompt) requires the LLM to return valid JSON matching the Pydantic model schema. For complex nested Pydantic models (models with 5+ nested optional fields), JSON schema complexity causes LLMs with weaker instruction following (GPT-3.5-turbo, smaller local models) to generate partially correct or empty JSON, which fails Pydantic validation and raises pydantic.ValidationError. The failure rate increases with schema complexity: simple flat models (3-5 string fields) have <5% parse failures; complex nested models with Union types have 15-30% failures even with GPT-4. The correct production strategy is to simplify the output schema to the minimum necessary fields, use instructor library with retry logic for complex schemas (instructor automatically retries with the validation error feedback), and monitor schema validation failure rates as a production metric.

The standard LlamaIndex assembly pattern for a legal document analysis system requiring source citation to paragraph level is: (1) PDFReader with page_break detection to split documents by page; (2) SentenceSplitter preserving paragraph boundaries with chunk_size=300, chunk_overlap=30; (3) store metadata: {document_title, page_number, paragraph_number, section_header} extracted via regex; (4) VectorStoreIndex with MetadataFilters for document scoping; (5) CitationQueryEngine with citation_chunk_size=512 for attribution; (6) post-process citations to include document_title + page_number + paragraph_number in the citation format. The critical legal requirement is that citations must be specific enough for a lawyer to locate the source in the original document: 'Document Title, p.15, para 3' is acceptable, but 'retrieved from knowledge base' is not. The citation quality directly determines whether the system meets legal discovery standards.

LlamaIndex's Evaluation module provides automated RAG quality measurement via RAGAs metrics computed using the LLM itself. LlamaIndex wraps Ragas metrics (faithfulness, answer_relevancy, context_recall, context_precision) via a EvaluationResult data class that stores per-query scores and explanations. The batch evaluation workflow: evaluator = FaithfulnessEvaluator(llm=gpt4); results = evaluator.evaluate_response(query=q, response=r, contexts=retrieved_nodes); result.passing is True/False; result.score is 0.0-1.0; result.feedback is the LLM's reasoning for the score. The key production use is regression testing: run the evaluation suite on every code change that affects retrieval or synthesis to detect quality regressions before deployment. A minimum of 50 diverse queries with gold-standard expected answers should constitute the regression test suite, balanced across easy and hard queries for the specific domain.

LlamaIndex's default text splitter respects sentence boundaries using the sentence-transformers SentenceSplitter algorithm, but this approach requires the sentence-transformers library to be installed. In minimal deployment environments (AWS Lambda with package size limits), the full sentence-transformers dependency (500MB+) may not be acceptable. The lightweight alternative is TokenTextSplitter which splits on token count boundaries without sentence awareness, available with only tiktoken as a dependency (~10MB). TokenTextSplitter produces lower-quality chunks (splitting mid-sentence) but requires 50x less disk space for the deployment package. The constraint is that different splitters produce incompatible chunk structures: switching from SentenceSplitter to TokenTextSplitter requires full re-ingestion because the node IDs and chunk boundaries change, making the old vector index incompatible with new embeddings generated for differently-chunked text.

LlamaIndex's integration with local LLMs via Ollama or LlamaCPP requires specifying the context_window parameter matching the model's actual context window, because LlamaIndex uses this value to calculate how many nodes to include in synthesis prompts. Setting context_window too high (e.g., 128000 for a model that actually supports only 8192 tokens) causes the synthesis step to include more nodes than the model can process, resulting in truncated context at the LLM level with no error from LlamaIndex. The LLM silently processes only the first context_window tokens and generates a response based on incomplete context. The correct configuration for local models: Ollama(model='llama3', context_window=8192, request_timeout=120.0). The request_timeout is equally important: local models on CPU can take 60-120 seconds for a generation, and the default 60-second timeout causes TimeoutError on slow hardware or for long responses.

The recommended LlamaIndex pattern for multi-modal document ingestion (PDFs with embedded images, presentations with slides) is a two-pass extraction strategy: first pass extracts text via standard PDFReader or LlamaParse; second pass uses a VLM (GPT-4V or Claude 3) to generate text descriptions of each image/figure. The image descriptions are stored as separate Document objects with metadata linking them to the original page, then indexed alongside the text chunks in a unified VectorStoreIndex. This enables queries like 'find charts showing Q3 revenue growth' to retrieve both text mentions and image descriptions of relevant charts. The implementation challenge is extracting images from PDFs: use pdfplumber.pages[n].images to get image bounding boxes and pymupdf.Page.get_image() to extract the actual image bytes. Images smaller than 100x100 pixels (decorative icons, page borders) should be filtered before VLM description to avoid polluting the index with noise.

LlamaIndex's LlamaParse service (cloud-based PDF parsing) vs. local PDF parsing (pdfplumber, PyMuPDF) represents a quality-vs-cost tradeoff: LlamaParse uses a specialized LLM to parse PDFs including scanned documents, complex tables, and multi-column layouts with high fidelity, at a cost of $0.003 per page. For a 1000-page corpus, LlamaParse costs $3 and produces near-perfect text extraction; PyMuPDF is free but produces garbled text for scanned PDFs, mis-ordered text for multi-column layouts, and incorrectly formatted tables. The decision point: use LlamaParse for customer-facing knowledge bases where document quality directly impacts user experience; use PyMuPDF for internal tools where imperfect extraction is acceptable. LlamaParse also returns structured markdown that preserves table formatting, enabling tables to be indexed as structured data rather than flattened text, improving Q&A accuracy on tabular content by 30-50%.

LlamaIndex's ContextChatEngine maintains conversation history by storing all past messages in memory, with no eviction policy by default. In a long-running chat session with 100+ turns, the conversation history grows to thousands of tokens and the LLM's context window is consumed by history rather than retrieved knowledge. The ContextChatEngine does not automatically summarize old turns when history exceeds a threshold. The correct production configuration is to use a ChatMemoryBuffer with explicit token_limit: chat_engine = CondensePlusContextChatEngine.from_defaults(index.as_retriever(), memory=ChatMemoryBuffer.from_defaults(token_limit=3000)). The ChatMemoryBuffer automatically truncates the oldest messages when the token limit is exceeded, keeping only the most recent context. Setting token_limit too low (< 500 tokens) causes the model to lose critical context from earlier in the conversation, degrading coherence in multi-turn sessions.

The standard LlamaIndex assembly for enterprise search over a mixed corpus (PDF, Word, HTML, email, Slack) is a multi-source ingestion pipeline with source-specific loaders and unified metadata normalization: each source type has its own loader (PDFReader, DocxReader, SimpleWebPageReader, GmailReader), each loader enriches nodes with source-specific metadata (pdf: page_number; email: from, to, date; slack: channel, thread_ts). A metadata normalization step standardizes all sources to a common schema: {source_type, author, date_iso, title, url_or_path}. This normalized metadata enables cross-source filtering (date range, author, source_type) in a single VectorStoreIndex. The key production challenge is author normalization: the same person appears as 'John Smith', 'J. Smith', 'jsmith@company.com', and '@jsmith' across sources; a name normalization step using string similarity or a lookup table is required before indexing for consistent author-based filtering.

LlamaIndex's NLSQLTableQueryEngine enables natural language querying of SQL databases by: (1) building a schema representation of the database tables from INFORMATION_SCHEMA; (2) generating SQL from the natural language query using the schema as context; (3) executing the SQL and formatting results as text. The key capability is table selection: for databases with 50+ tables, showing all tables to the LLM exceeds context limits. SQLTableNodeMapping with ObjectIndex builds an embedding-based table selector: the user's question is used to retrieve the most relevant tables (by semantic similarity to table descriptions), and only those tables' schemas are included in the SQL generation prompt. This selective schema inclusion enables NL-to-SQL on large databases with hundreds of tables at the cost of one embedding lookup per query. The SQLTableNodeMapping requires explicit table descriptions: provide accurate one-sentence descriptions for each table to enable semantic table selection.

LlamaIndex's node_postprocessors are applied to retrieved NodeWithScore objects AFTER retrieval and BEFORE synthesis, but they run synchronously in the main request thread even when aquery() is used. For computationally expensive postprocessors like SentenceTransformerRerank (which runs a cross-encoder model inference), the postprocessor blocks the asyncio event loop for 100-500ms if not offloaded to a thread pool. In a FastAPI application with concurrent users, this serializes the reranking step across all users, creating a queue. The correct implementation wraps the synchronous reranker in an async executor: override the async _apostprocess_nodes method in a custom subclass to call asyncio.get_event_loop().run_in_executor(None, self._postprocess_nodes, nodes, query_bundle). LLMRerank is I/O-bound (LLM API calls) and is naturally async; SentenceTransformerRerank is compute-bound (neural network inference) and requires explicit thread offloading.

LlamaIndex's document hash-based deduplication (in IngestionPipeline with docstore_strategy=UPSERTS) computes a hash from the document's text content and metadata combined. If two documents have the same text but different metadata (e.g., same PDF indexed twice with different file_path metadata due to a file rename or mount point change), the hash differs and both are ingested, creating duplicate embeddings in the vector store. Conversely, if document text changes but the doc_id stays the same (e.g., a versioned document where you track doc_id explicitly), the pipeline detects it as an existing document and skips re-embedding unless the hash also changed. The correct deduplication strategy for versioned documents is to set doc_id explicitly from a stable document identifier (document UUID from a CMS, not the file path), ensuring that document identity is tracked by business ID rather than file path or content hash, enabling updates to be detected when content changes under the same business ID.

The recommended LlamaIndex pattern for A/B testing retrieval strategies without code changes is to parameterize the retrieval strategy via a configuration object and log all query-response-retrieval triples to an evaluation database. The configuration object specifies: embedding_model, chunk_size, similarity_top_k, reranker_type, synthesis_mode. Each configuration variant is tested by routing a percentage of production traffic to it using a feature flag system. Quality metrics (user feedback thumbs up/down, engagement time, follow-up questions rate) are tracked per configuration variant. The evaluation database stores: query_id, config_variant, retrieved_node_ids, llm_response, user_feedback, latency_ms. Analysis of this database identifies which retrieval configuration produces the best user satisfaction at acceptable latency. This pattern enables data-driven RAG optimization without requiring offline benchmark creation.

LlamaIndex's automatic prompt compression (LongContextReorder postprocessor) reorders retrieved nodes to place the most relevant nodes at the beginning and end of the context, exploiting the 'lost in the middle' phenomenon where LLMs struggle to extract information from the middle of long contexts. However, this reordering changes the semantic flow of consecutive nodes from the same document, potentially disrupting context that relies on sequential reading order. For technical documentation where later chunks reference earlier definitions ('as described in step 3 above...'), reordering breaks the cross-chunk references. The correct production approach is to apply LongContextReorder only when nodes come from different documents (where order independence is safe), and to preserve sequential order for nodes from the same document (same doc_id). Check node.ref_doc_id before reordering and group by source document.
